右侧和左手页面的沟槽不一样。由于书籍绑定的方式，每个页面的“内部”都需要不同数量的“空白空间” - 这是在它绑定的书中中间的页面的一侧。出于盒子乳胶照顾，并在左手页面（绑定位于右侧）和右手页面（左侧的位置）上产生不同的浇口的PDF。
 使用单行，我定义了所有章节在右侧页面开始。这意味着有时插入空白页面，我真的很喜欢章节之间提供更多空间的方式，以及始终在右手页面上开始章节的一致性。
 打印书的目录可以在单行代码中生成，并且它会自动包含所有章节。通过更多的乳胶代码，TOC的确切外观可以为您的内容进行定制
 页眉和页脚可以严重定制，并且可以为不同的页面类型定制。以下选择是完全任意的，他们只是我想要的书。你想做什么，就可以做什么。我在To Stuff和Toc之后开始计算页码，然后我将页码添加到右手页面上，但不是在空白页上（仅从章节仅在右侧）而不是章节标题页面。我还在左手页面上添加了本书的标题，但再次在上面的“特殊”页面上。
 脚注是精美的方式处理，并在文本中生成一个小型的上标自动递增号，并且在一个小水平线后，脚注的实际正文在相应页面的底部呈现。这些结果可以定制（每章的数字，整个书籍末尾的全部脚注，也是整本书的末尾等）Pandoc与没有额外努力的乳胶脚注，也在电子书中创建可点击的链接，所有外部URL完美地工作。
note_en:
After years working as a Software Engineer I wanted to make my dreams come true, so I quit my desk job to spend my life adventuring around the world. I gambled that I would be able to scrape together a meagre living while enjoying a life I had dreamed of. After driving 40,000 miles through 17 countries from Alaska to Argentina, and then 54,000 miles through 35 countries around Africa, I learned, saw and experienced more than I ever dreamed possible. Five years of global adventures means I enough stories and lessons learned to fill multiple volumes, and I decided I wanted to do just that. I didn’t want the hassle of pitching publishers and all that goes along with that, so I went the self-publishing route, which became a whole new adventure.
 In this post I’ll run through all the details from the whys to the hows to a final recap with my thoughts about how my decisions worked out. Given I used to sling code, I’ll include a lot of code samples used to achieve my goals which you can use too. This post is not intended to teach you the nitty-gritty details of LaTeX, but rather to give a high(ish) level overview of what can be achieved with the tech-stack I settled on.
   Yes indeed. Amazon’s service  Kindle Direct Publishing makes the whole process of self-publishing much, much easier than most people realize. In essence you just upload a pdf of the interior of your book and a pdf containing the front & back cover and spine, and in a few days the book will be for sale, and you can order your own real-life bound book. With barely any extra work you can also submit an industry standard .epub file and your book will also be sold digitally for eReaders. It’s hard to describe the feeling of holding a published book with your name across the top, though I distinctly remember saying “Holy S**t, it’s a REAL book!”
  It’s  all up to you. The content, formatting, layout, spell checking and literally  everything else is under your direct control.
 You can publish anything you want. Maybe you’ve always wanted to write a book about the size of your cat’s furballs, the nifty light-up keyboard you designed and built or simply your journal entries. You can, and nobody will stop you.
 You don’t pay a single cent up front, because the whole thing is “print on demand”. When someone orders a book, Amazon print it, then send it to that customer. You don’t have to pre-purchase 500 or 50,000 copies and have them sitting on a shelf gathering dust while you stress about selling them to get your money back. If the book never sells a single copy, you pay nothing. (But you can order a few copies yourself to give your family   ) If and when the book does sell, Amazon handle everything and just deposit money into your bank account each month.
 You set your own prices (and therefore profit), within reason. Based on your choices on book size, color or black and white, number of pages and a few other things, Amazon calculate how much it costs to print and send to a customer. It’s your choice how far above that cost price you choose to sell the book for, thus defining your own profit per sale. You can play with Amazon’s  calculator here to see exactly the cost for a given book size/type.
  It’s  all up to you. The content, formatting, layout, spell checking and literally  everything else is under your direct control.
  If I was going to self-publish a book, I wanted it to look as professional as possible. While it’s possible to use MS Word to generate the pdf of the interior of the book, I personally think the results look homemade, and I can spot those books a mile away.
 I wanted to generate the pdf for the print book and the .epub file from the same source files. As a developer there’s nothing worse than copy-pasting back and forth, and there’s   no way I would tolerate having to fix typos or make edits in multiple places. I wanted to edit the content of the book in exactly  one place and generate the two different outputs from that.
 The whole process had to be automated – hopefully with one click or keystoke I would generate a finished pdf AND a fully compliant .epub file with no manual intervention.
  After a bunch of research I re-discovered the powerful and free high-quality typesetting language LaTeX, which I had used at University. The more I dug into it, the more I realized it’s a full-blown programming language, and it would certainly give me the pixel-perfect layout control I was looking for, and with  very little effort on my end I could create a book that looked as professional as the real thing.
 Reading around I came across  Pandoc, a free an open source document converter, often called the swiss army knife of document converters. I think that does a disservice, Pandoc is more like 26 and a half swiss army knives glued together, and it’s awesome. If you need to convert <document format A> to <document format B>, Pandoc has your back.
 So I decided to write the book in LaTeX which would generate the print-ready pdf, and then run the raw LaTeX files through Pandoc to generate an industry-standard .epub file for the electronic copy. That sounds easier than it is, but it’s not all that bad.
 NOTE: I certainly could have used Markdown or one of the many other document formats that became popular since I graduated in 2004, though I seriously doubt they can provide the level of control I get with LaTeX and the professional-grade layout I achieved. (I go into more detail on this later)
   Using the extremely-helpful “Createspace” LaTeX package (found  here), it’s straightforward enough to get a bare bones book up and running that is “Amazon compliant” in terms of margins and gutters and page sizes. I created a file called “The Road Chose Me – Volume 2.tex” which looks like:
  Line 3 gives the createspace package the details on the book. ( NOTE: Kindle Direct Publishing (KDP) was previously called Createspace, and now the two terms are more-or-less interchangeable)
 “pocket” means it’s 5.25″ x 8″ in size (There are  many  sizes to choose from, called ‘trim size’)
 “bleed” false means content does not extend to the very edges of the page (used more for photos/magazines)
 “cream” paper is the color of the paper I choose to print on (instead of white) (Which impacts thickness calculations)
 In just that one line I’ve given the createspace package all the info it needs to set the margins, gutters and a bunch of other stuff, and it will even provide warnings if I violate any of KDP’s rules. For example the warning you see on like 18 is because this tiny example book is only 18 pages in length, while KDP requires a minimum of 25 pages. I’d get similar warnings if I specified a custom gutter that was outside KDP’s mandatory minimums for a given number of pages. Nifty. (I could specific custom margins and gutters and other values, if I felt the need)
  Lines 12 and 14 include separate chapters of my book. Being a developer I wanted everything in separate source files, and a chapter-at-a-time felt like a really great way to break up the project. This also means when I’m in “writing” mode I don’t have to deal with any LaTeX code, I can just hammer out text and trust the definitions I’ve made in my master file will handle everything.
   With use of the Lettrine package I get some really nice drop caps at the start of each chapter, and by defining my own chapter headings and subheadings, I get this very neat result (from the pdf of the print book):
   In the same directory as my master LaTeX file I wrote a shell script to run Pandoc and a little extra
   Lines 3 and 4 are just removing the output from the last run so I start with a clean slate.
 Line 6 … well, yeah, I better explain that. Pandoc was never designed to parse something as complicated at createspace.sty (which is 917 lines of LaTeX code I can barely parse myself). If we leave it where Pandoc expects to find it, Pandoc will try to parse it and spew errors and problems. So the easy solution is just to rename it so Pandoc can’t find it, then later rename it back again (line 12). Pandoc simply ignores it and moves on without a problem.
 Line 8 actually invokes Pandoc to generate the output file (-o) of an .epub from the input file (-f) of the .tex document. I also give Pandoc the –epub-cover-image argument to a pretty jpg, and I use –epub-metadata to ensure the right metadata goes into the final .epub. Lastly I tell Pandoc to generate a Table Of Contents (–toc).
 Finally on line 14 I invoke Amazon’s “ KindleGen” program which turns the industry-standard .epub file into a proprietary Amazon .mobi file. I found it helpful to run this command as it will spit out errors if the metadata is not set correctly or if there are any other minor glitches in the .epub file that might cause issues later when you upload it to KDP.  NOTE: While writing this I just discovered Amazon no longer offer/support “KindleGen” and instead use “ Kindle Previewer“. I’ll have to look into that.
    So there you have it. We’ve generated a print-ready pdf file for the paperback edition, and an industry standard and validated .epub file for the digital copy. All done.
  Earlier I said I chose this tool chain because it’s extremely powerful and gives me a TON of flexibility on the layout to satisfy my OCD and make it  really professional. Let’s get into some of that now.  NOTE: From now on I won’t show complete code samples, but describe and show how I beefed up what I’ve shown thus far to achieve my goals.
  ( NOTE: I’m not a LaTeX guru, and I certainly can’t wrangle it to my needs nearly as well as I could a Java BookFactoryFactoryFactory. Chances are I’m about to use some of the wrong terminology, but hopefully I can convey the major points in a way you can understand and follow.)
 Also a lot of what I do from here on (and the use of Lettrine above) require the use of LaTeX packages which can be thought of as extensions, or collections of bits n pieces of layout code/control that do cool things. They don’t always have the most verbose names, though with some googling and poking around it’s not too hard to figure out what you need to achieve your goals. On MacOS I’m using  TeX Live Utility to install and manage LaTeX packages, which makes the whole thing fairly painless. Whenever I use code from the LaTeX Stack Exchange I quickly get an error about a missing package – so then I use TeX Live to install it and I’m all good. As for packages I’ve shown you how the “Lettrine” package does cool drop caps, the “caption” package is handy for captioning images to your liking and I’ll let you guess what the package “multicol” does. There are mountains of packages, and there is virtually always more than one way to skin the cat to achieve the particular layout you’re looking for. Spend some time searching on  tex.stackexchange.com to see many, many code samples and answers that will help you achieve what you’re looking for.
  It turns out LaTeX is more-or-less a full-blown programming language, and therefore you can do powerful things like conditionals. I realized there were times I wanted different results for the print book vs. the eBook, so in my main file “The Road Chose Me – Volume 2.tex” I added a conditional (toggle) called “ebook”. Toggles come from the package etoolbox.
  And anywhere I want in the LaTeX code I can check if that toggle is true or false and do different things based on the state. In the master file I always leave it false, and toggle it true then false in the shell script ./makeEBook.sh like this
 (No comments about my sed magic please. I was sitting on a beach in Mozambique when I wrote that, and I’m proud of it!)
 So when regular LaTeX and pdf2latex run they see the conditional as false, but when Pandoc runs it sees the conditional as true, therefore taking a different code path within the LaTeX code. Yes, Pandoc understands these toggles and parses them correctly.
   Like all good programming languages, LaTeX lets you define your own functions (usually they’re called macros, but there are other names for them too, depending on the specifics. Nevermind).
 Let’s say for example I want to have a nice way to have some kind of pretty break in the text, to signify some kind of natural break. In the eBook (which is just HTML after all) I would just use a horizontal rule, the good ‘ol <hr> tag. But in LaTeX I want to do something different, though I don’t want to have the burden of thinking about which is which when I’m in the flow of writing a chapter.
 So I’ll create a macro to do it for me, and I’ll use that eBook conditional to produce a different result in the print-ready pdf and the eBook.
 I’ll call this macro “textbreak”, and I can call it in any of my chapters like this
 So it’s nice n easy and doesn’t snap me out of the “flow” of writing.
 I must define that macro in my main file, “The Road Chose Me – Volume 2.tex” and it must be between \begin{document} and \mainmatter, and before I use it. I also have the macro defined after the toggle is defined. My new macro is defined using the ‘\newcommand’ like this:
 So by writing my own macro and checking the conditional I now have slightly different output in the print-ready pdf and the .epub file.
  I don’t really know what it’s called in LaTeX land, but you can overload existing functions (macros) just like in OO programming. Earlier I showed the use of the “Lettrine” package to get nifty drop caps on the first word of each chapter. Again, I want the output to be slightly different in the two different output formats. So although I just call “Lettrine” with arguments in each chapter, it actually calls my overloaded version of that function. To do this you use the ‘\renewcommand’ like so:
 My function overload specifies three parameters must be passed, and you’ll notice my overloaded version of the macro only exists when the ebook toggle is true. So for the print-ready pdf LaTeX just uses the standard definition, but for the .epub file Pandoc will use my overloaded version.  Now we’re getting some powerful tools I expect from a programming language!
    In another extremely powerful feature, it’s possible to define a block of “verbatim” code that will pass right though Pandoc as-is, using a lua script. The final .epub file is just HTML, so that’s what I choose to pass through. My overloaded “lettrine” actually looks like this, using the CSS style to achieve an effect similar to “Lettrine” drop caps.
 So my overloaded lettrine says to output parameters 2 and 3 wrapped in some basic HTML and a line before and after that is ‘%%%HTML’. (If you’re super curious, parameters 2 and 3 are the actual letters of the word being made fancy, parameter 1 is just telling the original Lettrine package exactly how to format it – see the code sample up higher)
 Now in my shell script ./makeEBook.sh, I tell Pandoc to use a lua filter while parsing, like so
 and finally the file rawHTMLFilter.lua contains:    Which I shamelessly copied from a Pandoc example   But it basically says if the pattern of ‘%%%HTML’ is matched, then just pass it through Pandoc as raw HTML, so we get the result we want in the final .epub file.
 I also use the eBook toggle (conditional) to include color images in the eBook version, but only Black and White images in the print-ready pdf. That way I can control the contrast more directly to get the best print results.
 ( NOTE: Unfortunately Amazon’s KDP service CAN NOT print a book that is partially B&W and partially Color. It’s an all-or-nothing deal, and for my 370 book, all color would be prohibitively expensive, so I have to stick with B&W for all the text AND the images)
  There are a lot more powerful things we can do with this tool chain, including unpacking the .epub file to hand massage it how we please. An .epub file is just zipped html documents after all.
 As I said earlier I’m a bit OCD when it comes to layout and exactly how things look, though when it comes to an .epub file we just have to accept certain limitations. We can’t control the font size or where page breaks will occur on the thirteen millions eReaders that might be used to read our book, but I did go in and change a few things like removing blank lines after HTML ordered lists, stopping copyright symbols forcing a line break, and making sure footnotes were not inside <p> tags, creating a ton of blank lines where I didn’t want them
 With some extra code in the ./makeEBook.sh shell script I unpack the .epub file, massage some html, swap in custom master CSS document and then bundle the whole thing back up into a .epub file.
 The code to do this is more nasty sed and perl magic, so I’ll just give you an idea here…
   I mentioned earlier that I feel LaTeX provides much more control and power over the final layout than other document formats can. This is most noticeable in the print-ready pdf which becomes the printed book. Here are a few points to consider if you’re choosing a document format (or language.. or whatever they’re called) if you want to print a book
 Gutters are not the same on right and left-hand pages. Because of the way books are bound, a different amount of “blank space” is required on the “inside” of each page – the side of the page that is in the middle of the book where it’s bound. Right out of the box LaTeX takes care of this and generates a pdf that has different gutters on left hand pages (where the binding is on the right) and right hand pages (where the binding is on the left).
 With a single line I defined that all chapters start on a right hand page. This means sometimes a blank page is inserted, and I really like the way this gives a little more space between chapters, and the consistency of always having chapters start on right hand pages.
 A Table Of Contents for the print book can be generated in a single line of code, and it automatically includes all Chapters. With more LaTeX code the exact look of the TOC can be customized to your heart’s content
 Headers and footers can be heavily customized, and they can be customized for different page types. The following choices are completely arbitrary, and they’re just what I wanted for my book. You can do whatever you want. I start counting page numbers after the into stuff and after the TOC, and then I add page numbers to the top of right hand pages, BUT NOT on blank pages (from chapters being on the right only) and NOT on chapter heading pages. I also add the title of the book on the top of left hand pages, but again NOT on “special” pages as above.
 Footnotes are handled beautifully, and produce a small superscript auto-incrementing number in the text, and the actual body of the footnote is rendered on the bottom of the corresponding page after a small horizontal line. These results can be customized (numbers per chapter, all footnotes at the end of a chapter / end of the entire book, etc. etc.) Pandoc parses the LaTeX footnotes with no additional effort, also creating clickable links in the eBook, and all external URLs work perfectly.
title: 我使用乳胶和潘约自我发布了平装和电子书

简短版本：第一批关于雷追踪的书籍，从1989年开始介绍雷追踪，现在可以免费下载。您可以获得PDF或DJVU版本。请注意，PDF已更新了一个错误修复（到目前为止），目前在1.1版本。
 较长的版本：今年夏天，利用Pete Shirley释放了他的三本介绍性迷你书籍，那么Matt Pharr免费释放了物理上的渲染，我问安德鲁Glassner如果他可以获得释放经典介绍的权利射线跟踪。他是游戏，用作者清算它，在elestvier追查合适的人，询问，通过文书工作，释放！他只是写了我：
 我刚刚收到了缩略追踪的概论的伯爵来级版本。我猜合同是我的名字，因为我现在拥有权利！我特此授予您公开分享上述书籍或其手稿的许可，或其稿件，使用一个或多个人类设计中的一个或多个字体，在一般阅读乐趣的网站上使用一个或多个字体进行电气编码和呈现阅读公众。去吧！
 所以我去了。享受！是的，这是古老的。但令人惊讶的是，它仍然是一堆方式的精细介绍 - 数学不会腐烂，然后从后面的许多比特仍然使用 - 随机取样，边界卷，自适应超级采样等（Matt Pharr同意） 。安德鲁的关于这本书的页面（以及关于封面的一些历史）在这里。
 我，我预计这将减少到我的年度版税里 - 我去年在这本书上获得了6.22美元 - 但是啊，我为更好的牺牲而造成了更好的牺牲，也谦虚。如果您认为免费书籍是一个令人毛骨悚然的，您可以随时在亚马逊的疯狂页面上购买副本 - 窃取（在某人的部分）以588.23美元。普通的亚马逊书籍页面不是那么邦克。
 更新：安德鲁被问到这本书的许可证是什么。我们讨论了它，他选择了第4.0届国际（CC）许可证，最自由的CC许可证。引用主要位：
   归因 - 您必须提供适当的信用，提供许可证的链接，并指出是否进行了更改。您可以以任何合理的方式这样做，但不能以任何方式建议许可人赞同您或您的使用 
没有额外的限制 - 您可能不会申请合法限制其他人的法律术语或技术措施，以便在做许可证的情况下做任何事情。  如果由一些奇迹，您使用的是这本书的第一个打印，我已经制作了一个错误的页面。 除了指出，所有这些错误都在PDF和DJVU版本中修复。
note_en:
Short version: the first book on ray tracing,  An Introduction to Ray Tracing, from 1989, is now free to download. You can get the   PDF or   DJVU version. Note that the PDF has been updated with  one erratum fix (so far), and currently is at version 1.1.
 Longer version: with Pete Shirley releasing for free  his three introductory mini-books on ray tracing this summer, then Matt Pharr  releasing the book  Physically Based Rendering for free, I asked Andrew Glassner if he could get the rights to release the classic  An Introduction to Ray Tracing. He was game, clearing it with the authors, tracing down the right person at Elsevier to ask, going through the paperwork, and – freed! He just wrote me:
 I’ve just now received the countersigned rights release for  An Introduction to Ray Tracing. I guess the contract was in my name, because I now own the rights! I hereby grant you permission to publicly share the aforementioned book, or the manuscript thereof, or an electronified version of the manuscript thereof, electrically encoded and presented using one or more typefaces of human design, upon a website for the general reading pleasure of the general reading public. Go for it!
 So going for it I am. Enjoy! Yes, it’s ancient. But, surprisingly, it’s still a fine introduction in a bunch of ways – math doesn’t rot, and many of the bits from back then are still used today – stochastic sampling, bounding volumes, adaptive supersampling, etc. (Matt Pharr  agrees). Andrew’s page about the book (and some history about the cover) is  here.
 Me, I expect this will cut into my yearly royalties – I earned $6.22 last year on the book – but, ah, the giant sacrifices I make for The Greater Good, and so humble, too. If you consider free books a travesty, you can always buy a copy off  this insane page on Amazon – a steal (on someone’s part) at $588.23. The  normal Amazon book page is not so bonkers.
 Update: Andrew was asked what sort of license the book is under. We discussed it and he chose the  Attribution 4.0 International (CC BY 4.0) license, the most liberal of the CC licenses. To quote the main bits:
   Attribution — You must give  appropriate credit, provide a link to the license, and  indicate if changes were made. You may do so in any reasonable manner, but not in any way that suggests the licensor endorses you or your use
 No additional restrictions — You may not apply legal terms or  technological measures that legally restrict others from doing anything the license permits.
   If by some miracle you use the first printing of the book, I’ve made  an errata page. All these errors are fixed in the PDF and DJVU versions, except as noted.

Flipper Zero是Tamagotchi样式的Pentesters和Geek的便携式多功能工具。它喜欢黑客数字的东西，如无线电协议，访问控制系统，硬件等。它的完全开源和可自定义，所以您可以以无论您喜欢的方式扩展。
  Flipper零的想法是将所有关键的硬件工具组合在一起，需要在GO上进行黑客攻击。 Flipper受到Pwnagotchi项目的启发，但与黑客其他DIY板不同，Flipper旨在掌握日常使用的便利 - 它具有一个强大的案例，方便的按钮和形状，因此没有脏的PCB或刮刀。 Flipper转变为游戏，提醒你应该始终很有趣。
  Flipper Zero是一块微小的硬件，具有真正喜欢破解的网络海豚的好奇个性。它可以在现实生活中与数字系统交互，而在攻击时会增长。使用GPIO引脚翻转任何类型的访问控制系统，RFID，无线电协议，并执行硬件黑客。
 Flipper零是完全自主的，可以从5位方向焊盘控制，而无需额外的设备，例如计算机或智能手机。菜单中可以使用常用脚本和攻击。有关更多控制，您可以通过USB连接到鳍状芯。我们决定在阳光下完美可见，而不是TFT / IPS / OLED，而是在阳光下完美可见，并且在阳光下具有超低功耗400NA。
 这是广泛的无线设备和访问控制系统的操作范围，例如车库门遥感，动力障碍，物联网传感器和远程无钥匙系统。 Flipper具有集成的433MHz天线和CC1101芯片，使其成为能够高达100米的强大收发器。
                Flipper Zero对流行的遥控算法具有集成的解码器：Keeloq，来，门汉等，所以您可以分析一个未知的无线电系统来弄清楚底层协议。它可以记录和存储无线电数据的样本以稍后在计算机上分析，以及重放保存的样本。许多设备，如遥控器，门铃，传感器和无线电套接字，Don＆＃39; t完全使用任何加密 - 在这种情况下，即使协议没有识别出来，鳍状公司也可以重放信号。哦，还有一件事 - 鳍状公司使用433 MHz与其他脚蹼与其他脚蹼相互沟通，就像一个tamagochi，所以你可以制作一些网络海豚朋友:)
        CC1101是一种用于非常低功耗的无线应用的通用收发器。它支持各种类型的数字调制，如2-FSK，4-FSK，GFSK和MSK，以及OOK和Flexible Bay Shighing。您可以在应用程序中执行任何数字通信，例如连接到IOT设备和访问控制系统。 
这种类型的卡广泛用于世界各地的旧访问控制系统。它＆＃39; s漂亮的愚蠢，仅存储n字节ID并没有身份验证机制，允许被任何人读取，克隆和模仿。一个125 kHz天线位于鳍状肢底部 - 它可以读取EM-4100和HID Prox卡，将它们保存到内存以稍后模拟。您也可以通过手动输入其ID来效仿卡。此外，翻转业主可以远程交换卡转储。
 Flipper Zero具有内置的NFC模块（13.56 MHz）。随着125kHz模块，它将鳍状度转换为在低频（LF）和高频（HF）范围内运行的终极RFID器件。 NFC模块支持所有主要标准，例如NXP Mifare。它与125 kHz模块相比相同，允许您与支持NFC的设备进行交互，读取，写和模拟HF标签，甚至使用具有可写UID的特殊Mifare 1K卡互补卡。
 Flipper Zero具有内置蓝牙低能量模块。与其他翻转无线功能一样，我们将提供一个开源库，用于向社区的应用程序添加Flipper支持。 Full Ble Support允许Flipper零作为主机和外围设备，允许您同时将鳍状公司连接到第三方设备和智能手机。我们的移动开发人员正在设计官方iOS和Android应用程序，让您释放较大的屏幕和更大的控制。
 红外发射器可以传输信号以控制电子设备，例如电视，空调，立体声系统等。 Flipper具有用于电源和音量控制的公共电视供应商命令序列的内置库。此库由翻转社区用户不断更新，将新信号上传到Flipper的IR远程数据库。
      Flipper Zero也具有IR接收器，可以接收信号并将其另存为库，因此您可以将任何现有的遥控器存储到稍后发送命令，并将其上载到公共IR远程数据库以与其他翻转用户共享。
 Flipper Zero可以通过熟悉的Arduino IDE或Platformio软件编写自己的代码来扩展功能。将草图上传到Flippers比与普通的Arduino板上更加复杂。您的代码可以使用所有内置的翻转器硬件：内置显示屏打印文本和绘制图像，按钮导航，用于子1GHz通信的无线电模块，RFID模块用于接近卡，IR模块用于红外通信的IR模块和GPIO引脚，用于将功能扩展为定制或DIY模块。顺便说一句，不要担心填补船上的内存 - 鳍状公司的闪存存储比Arduino Mega更多。
 Flipper Zero可以将代码作为插件运行，与基本的Arduino板不同，存储多个程序旁，与自己的固件一起。因此，任何代码都可以从插件菜单运行，而不每次需要时上传它。如果您的代码冻结或崩溃，则害怕打破某些东西 - 基本固件总是在那里。 
有很多繁重的数据鳍状件必须存储：遥控器，信号数据库，词典，图像资产，日志等。所有这些数据都可以存储在SD卡上，以及用户插件。 SD插槽将具有推送式连接器，因此该卡将可靠地固定在内部并赢得＆＃39; t伸出。 Flipper Zero将支持任何FAT32格式的microSD卡来存储您的资产，以便您永远不必担心内存会用完。 Flipper零不需要该卡运行，并且不包括在内。
 Flipper Zero是硬件黑客，固件闪烁，调试和模糊的多功能工具。它可以连接到任何使用GPIO的硬件连接它，以便使用按钮，运行自己的代码并打印调试消息到LCD显示屏。它也可以用作UART / SPI / I2C / etc适配器的常规USB。
                内置5V和3.3V电源引脚。从内置按钮和显示器控制，无需PC。
 Flipper Zero可以模拟USB从设备，并连接到像常规输入设备，例如HID键盘或以太网适配器，就像USB Ducky一样。您可以为键盘编写自己的有效载荷，以键入任何键序列，并在目标设备上运行USB模糊攻击。
 Flipper Zero具有内置的1线连接器，可读取IButton（AKA DS1990A，触摸存储器或达拉斯键）触点键。这项旧技术仍然广泛应用于世界各地。它使用1-Wire协议，该协议不具有任何身份验证。 Flipper可以轻松读取这些键，将ID存储到内存中，将ID写入空白键并模拟钥匙本身。 Flipper Zero在拐角处具有独特的接触式垫设计 - 其形状作为读卡器和探头同时连接到IButton插座。这种模式也可以方便地静静地拦截1线数据线。
    型号：STM32WB55 ARM Cortex-M4 32位64 MHz（应用处理器）ARM Cortex-M0 + 32 MHz（网络处理器）闪光：1024 KB SRAM：256 KB
        芯片：TI CC1101 TX功率：12 dBm最大频段：●300-348 MHz●387-464 MHz●779-928 MHz 
尺寸：100 x 40 x 25 mm重量：102克材料：PC工作温度：0〜50°C  芯片：ST25R3916频率：13.56 MHz支持卡：●ISO-14443A / B●NXPMIFARE®Classic/ Ultlight / Desfire / Etc●Felica™●NFC论坛协议  本网站使用cookie以确保您获得最佳体验。 阅读我们的隐私政策  关闭
note_en:
Flipper Zero is a portable multi-tool for pentesters and geeks in a Tamagotchi-like body. It loves hacking digital stuff, such as radio protocols, access control systems, hardware and more. It's fully open-source and customizable, so you can extend it in whatever way you like.
  The idea of Flipper Zero is to combine all the phreaking hardware tools you'd need for hacking on the go. Flipper was inspired by pwnagotchi project, but unlike other DIY boards for hackers, Flipper is designed with the convenience of everyday usage in mind — it has a robust case, handy buttons and shape, so there are no dirty PCBs or scratchy pins. Flipper turns hacking into a game, reminding you that hacking should always be fun.
  Flipper Zero is a tiny piece of hardware with a curious personality of a cyber-dolphin who really loves to hack. It can interact with digital systems in real life and grow while you are hacking. Flip any kind of access control system, RFID, radio protocol and perform hardware hacks using GPIO pins.
 Flipper Zero is completely autonomous and can be controlled from a 5-Position directional pad without additional devices, such as computers or smartphones. Common scripts and attacks are available from the menu.    For more control, you can connect to Flipper via USB. Instead of a TFT/IPS/OLED, we decided to build in a cool old-school LCD screen, which is perfectly visible in sunlight and has an ultra-low power consumption of 400nA with the backlight turned off.
 This is the operating range for a wide class of wireless devices and access control systems, such as garage door remotes, boom barriers, IoT sensors and remote keyless systems.    Flipper has an integrated 433MHz antenna, and a CC1101 chip, which makes it a powerful transceiver capable of  up to 100 meters range.
                Flipper Zero has an integrated decoder for popular remote control algorithms: Keeloq, Came, Doorhan and others, so you can analyze an unknown radio system to figure out the underlying protocol.    It can record and store the samples of radio data to analyze later on a computer, as well as replay saved samples. Many devices, such as remotes, doorbells, sensors and radio sockets, don't use any encryption at all — in this case, Flipper can replay the signal , even if the protocol wasn't recognized.    Oh, and one more thing — Flipper uses 433 MHz to communicate with other Flippers out there like a Tamagochi, so you can make some cyber-dolphin friends :)
        CC1101 is a universal transceiver designed for very low-power wireless applications. It supports various types of digital modulations such as 2-FSK, 4-FSK, GFSK and MSK, as well as OOK and flexible ASK shaping. You can perform any digital communication in your applications such as connecting to IoT devices and access control systems.
 This type of card is widely used in old access control systems around the world. It's pretty dumb, stores only an N-byte ID and has no authentication mechanism, allowing it to be read, cloned and emulated by anyone. A 125 kHz antenna is located on the bottom of Flipper — it can read EM-4100 and HID Prox cards, save them to memory to emulate later.   You can also emulate cards by entering their IDs manually. Moreover, Flipper owners can exchange card dumps remotely.
 Flipper Zero has a built-in NFC module (13.56 MHz). Along with the 125kHz module, it turns Flipper into an ultimate RFID device operating in both Low Frequency (LF) and High Frequency (HF) ranges. The NFC module supports all the major standards, such as NXP Mifare.  It works pretty much the same as the 125 kHz module, allowing you to interact with NFC-enabled devices, read, write and emulate HF tags and even clone cards using special Mifare 1K cards with a writable UID.
 Flipper Zero has a built-in Bluetooth Low Energy module. As with other Flipper wireless features, we will be providing an open source library for adding Flipper support to community-made apps.  Full BLE support allows Flipper Zero to act as both a host and a peripheral device, allowing you to connect your Flipper to 3rd-party devices and a smartphone simultaneously.  Our mobile developers are designing official iOS and Android apps to let you unleash Flipper's potential with a larger screen and greater control.
 The infrared transmitter can transmit signals to control electronics such as TVs, air conditioners, stereo systems and more.   Flipper has a built-in library of common TV vendor command sequences for power and volume control. This library is constantly updated by Flipper community users uploading new signals to Flipper’s IR Remote database.
      Flipper Zero also has an IR receiver that can receive signals and save then to the library, so you can store any of your existing remotes to transmit commands later, and upload to the public IR Remote database to share with other Flipper users.
 Flipper Zero can be extended functionality by your own code written with a familiar  Arduino IDE or  PlatformIO software. Uploading sketches to Flipper is as easy no more complicated than with a regular Arduino board.    Your code can use all built-in Flipper hardware: the built-in display to print text and draw images, the buttons to navigate, the Radio module for Sub-1Ghz communication, the RFID module for proximity cards, the IR module for infrared communication and GPIO pins for extending the functionality with custom or DIY modules.   By the way, don't worry about filling up the onboard memory — Flipper has four times more flash storage than an Arduino Mega.
 Flipper Zero can run your code as a plugin, storing multiple programs alongside its own firmware, unlike basic Arduino boards. Thus, any code can be run from the plugins menu without uploading it every time you need it. And don't be afraid to break something if your code freezes or crashes — the base firmware is always there.
 There is lots of heavy data Flipper has to store: remotes codes, signal databases, dictionaries, image assets, logs and more. All this data can be stored on an SD card, as well as user plugins.  The SD slot will have a push-push type connector, so the card will be reliably secured inside and won't stick out. Flipper Zero will support any FAT32 formatted microSD card to store your assets so you’ll never have to worry the memory will run out. The card is not required for Flipper Zero to operate and is not included.
 Flipper Zero is as a versatile tool for hardware hacking, firmware flashing, debugging and fuzzing. It can be connected to any piece of hardware using GPIO to control it with buttons, run your own code and print debug messages to the LCD display. It can also be used as a regular USB to UART/SPI/I2C/etc adapter.
                Built-in 5V and 3.3V power pins. Control from built-in buttons and display, no PC required.
 Flipper Zero can emulate USB slave devices and connect to a computer like a regular input device, such as an HID keyboard or an Ethernet adapter, just like USB Ducky. You can write your own payload for the keyboard to type any key sequence, and run USB fuzzing attacks on a target device.
 Flipper Zero has a built-in 1-Wire connector to read iButton (aka DS1990A, Touch Memory or Dallas key) contact keys. This old technology is still widely used around the world. It uses the 1-Wire protocol that doesn't have any authentication. Flipper can easily read these keys, store IDs to the memory, write IDs to blank keys and emulate the key itself.    Flipper Zero has a unique contact pad design on the corner — its shape works as a reader and a probe to connect to iButton sockets at the same time. This mode is also handy for silently intercepting the 1-Wire data line.

阿拉斯加将成为全国第一州，为16岁及以上的任何人开放Covid-19疫苗，官员周二宣布，盖上速度推出的射门，这些射门将资本化为数万种额外的剂量，由部落医疗保健提供者提供管理。
 GOV.Mike Dunleavy宣布了周二下午的准备好声明中扩大了资格，并于晚上举办了一份新闻发布会。官员说，它是有效的。住在阿拉斯加的任何人或工作的人都是有资格的。
 “我对阿拉斯加的回应不可能，”该声明引用了Dunleavy说。 “从第一个提供广泛测试的国家，以维持该国​​最低的死亡率之一，向每一个愿意的阿拉斯加推出疫苗接种，我们通过共同努力来到这里。”
  前几天的卫生官员暗示他们可能采取如此一步，这表明疫苗犹豫和关于以前扩大的资格指南的困惑是在整个国家未填补的任命。
 大约四分之一的阿拉斯加人已经收到了至少一剂Covid-19疫苗，使国家成为该国最高的疫苗接种率之一。在许多农村社区中，疫苗接种率远远超过50％，有一些村庄击中90％或更高。
 阿拉斯加州农村西部的十几个社区达到了50％以上的初级疫苗接种率，而且每个来自Norton Sound Health Corp.的图形，部分地区部落医疗保健提供者：pic.twitter .com / ya3exvxhvz.
 -  Nat Herz（@Nat_Herz）3月9日，2021年3月9日 
阿拉斯加的国家赞助商分销努力从其部落医疗伙伴的主要促进了，通过与联邦政府的独特伙伴关系分配了单独的疫苗货物 - 旨在认识到部落的主权地位和Covid-19的不成比例的影响阿拉斯加本地人。
  一系列国家新闻网点拥有并行疫苗接种努力的成功，这些疫苗接种努力使得灌木丛，雪地训练和船只在冬季深处偏离偏远的阿拉斯加社区。
  直到上周，国家有限的疫苗接种，教师和50岁及以上的人民在某些前线的必要位置工作或遭受某些预先存在的健康状况。
 但上周，官员宣布将射门占据射门数量的重大推动，疫苗接种将向一个远更广泛的群体开放：所有必要的工人，任何55岁及以上的人和任何有健康状况的人从Covid-19严重疾病的风险很高。
  尽管如此，虽然在该公告之后，数百项任命将在锚地和州周围的其他地方未填充。
 现在预约将向任何人开放16个，虽然16岁和17岁的人只有资格获得药品公司辉瑞制造的疫苗，但市场上的另外两个其他疫苗仅适用于18岁及以上的人。
 您可以通过国家疫苗网站找到有关疫苗资格和书籍预约的更多信息。

Alaska will become the first state in the country to open COVID-19 vaccinations to anyone 16 and older, officials announced Tuesday, capping a swift rollout of the shots that’s capitalized on tens of thousands of extra doses shipped to and administered by tribal health care providers.
 Gov. Mike Dunleavy announced the expanded eligibility in a prepared statement Tuesday afternoon, with a news conference scheduled for later in the evening. It’s effective immediately, officials said. Anyone who lives or works in Alaska is eligible.
 “I couldn’t be prouder of Alaska’s response,” the statement quoted Dunleavy as saying. “From being the first state to offer widespread testing, to maintaining one of the lowest mortality rates in the country, to rolling out vaccinations to every willing Alaskan, we got here by working together.”
  Health officials in previous days had hinted that they might take such a step, suggesting that vaccine hesitancy and confusion about previously broadened eligibility guidelines were leaving appointments unfilled across the state.
 About a quarter of Alaskans have received at least one dose of COVID-19 vaccine, giving the state one of the highest vaccination rates in the country. In many rural communities, vaccination rates far exceed 50%, with some villages hitting 90% or higher.
 More than a dozen communities in rural Western Alaska have hit first-dose vaccination rates of more than 50%, and some as high as 90%, per this graphic from Norton Sound Health Corp., the regional tribal health care provider:  pic.twitter.com/yA3exvxHvZ
— Nat Herz (@Nat_Herz)  March 9, 2021
 Alaska’s state-sponsored distribution effort has seen a major boost from its tribal health care partners, which have been allotted separate shipments of vaccine through a unique partnership with the federal government — aimed at recognizing both tribes’ sovereign status and COVID-19’s disproportionate impact on Alaska Native people.
  A series of national news outlets have featured the successes of the parallel vaccination efforts, which have leveraged  bush   planes, snowmachines and boats to get doses to remote Alaska communities in the depths of winter.
  Up until last week, the state limited vaccinations to health care workers, teachers, and people aged 50 and older who worked in certain front-line essential positions or suffered from certain pre-existing health conditions.
 But last week, buoyed by a major boost in the number of shots being shipped to the state, officials announced that vaccinations would open to a far broader group: all essential workers, anyone 55 and over and anyone with a health condition that could put them at high risk of serious illness from COVID-19.
  Even after that announcement, though, hundreds of appointments were going unfilled in Anchorage and elsewhere around the state.
 Now appointments will be open to anyone 16 and over, although 16- and 17-year-olds will only be eligible for the vaccine manufactured by drug company Pfizer, as the two other vaccines on the market are only authorized for people 18 and older.
 You can find more information about vaccine eligibility and book appointments through the  state’s vaccine website.
title: 阿拉斯加将是美国的第一个州，为所有成年人提供Covid-19疫苗
title_en: Alaska will be first state in U.S. to offer Covid-19 vaccines to all adults

    我学到的一个地方是一个小组聊天，我和我的朋友Dror Poleg和Ben卷。德罗斯撰写了关于工作，城市和金融的历史和未来，以及作曲家的创始人和首席执行官的Ben是我所知道的两个最聪明的人。
 本，三人组织的最具技术（其次是罗兰，然后是我），也恰好是一位出色的作家。 2月下旬，他发布了作曲家宣言，以制定投资作为创造性的努力的案例。它是针对这两种类型的人来定制的，他们读不到 - 投资者和科技人士 - 你应该阅读：
  所以当Ben发短信给我们关于Excel的力量有多低估的时候，我让他向我写下它。他向我的眼睛睁开眼睛，我不认识的产品中都存在于我在职业生涯的形成岁月中花费的每一个醒着的时间。我们会尽力为您做同样的事情。
     在流行的营销书炼金术中，罗里·萨瑟兰写道，“一张电子表格没有奇迹空间。”我们无法更加不同意。
   但是有一个软件产品于1985年出生，我们许多人甚至在父母的眼中闪烁，那么居住在自己的类别：它是老的，但我们喜欢它，我们总是会，你必须撬开它我们的寒冷，死了，手指。当然，该产品是Microsoft Excel。
  任何在金融或咨询工作的人都在努力，学会了在数千小时的练习和改进中。他们是否意识到与否，他们在没有代码运动起飞之前，他们正在成为程序员，或者至少是禁令的从业者。 “熟练在Microsoft Office Suite”是如此毫无意义，即它成为一个模因，但能够弯曲一个特定的办公程，Excel，一个人的意志是荣誉的徽章。 
但是，持久，激情的产品的热情，甚至不是它最独特的属性。 Excel最持久的影响延伸超出了电子表格本身。
 Excel可能是有史以来最具影响力的软件。这是史蒂夫乔治的骑自行车的规范示例，赋予其用户使用通常为专业软件工程师保留的计算超级大国。与这些超级大国一起武装，用户可以以谦逊的电子表格的形式创建完全功能的软件程序，以解决看似无限数量的域中的问题。这些程序通常作为域特定应用的高保真原型，只是乞求以更具优雅的形式推向市场。
 如果您希望看到B2B软件的未来，请查看今天的电子表格中的Excel用户在一起。 Excel的成功启发了创建软件，其组合企业价值DWARFS独自的Excel。几十年来，Excel为B2B软件行业设定了广阔的路线图，将继续持续多年：
 切断的excel。通过在Excel中的工作，并尝试在更优化的目的内置的B2B软件中完成工作，建立了数百名B2B初创公司。每次听到企业家都说，“我们正在用目的内置的软件取代SILED电子表格和过时的流程，”你实时听到了卓越的excel。许多流行的SaaS应用程序属于此类别。然而，尽管存在“不捆绑”，但Excel保持更强大。
 灵感来自Excel。随着胜利的是，振兴创造企业家在使Excel蜱虫中更深入看起来。冒险建设者正在创建不挤出Excel的新软件，而是由Excel启发。 Excel的可用性和灵活性的平衡可以在流行的没有代码和超过三十年创建的低码产品中找到，因为Excel首先进行了屏幕。这种灵感来源不太直接，更多的元;关于重新创建任何在Excel中发生的混凝土的内容，以及更多关于捕获所谓的卓越事项的本质。
 我们喜欢Excel，每个人都读到这可能是喜欢Excel，仍然，它的影响深刻被忽视。今天，我们将通过覆盖来充分欣赏它：
    我们有史蒂夫乔布斯感谢Microsoft Excel，而Microsoft Excel感谢Apple。电子表格软件是Mac和Home PC的第一个真正杀手应用程序，Mac的图形界面有助于将电子表格带到群众。这两者推动了其他人的增长。 
Excel不是第一个数字电子表格。当HBS学生Dan Bricklin不得不在手工或学校的主机上做出案例研究之间的电子表格之间时，他就像许多企业家一样，意识到必须是更好的方式。他于1978年推出了Visicalc，一个“可见计算器”。计算机员工在1980年后的两年后，Supercalc。同年，Mitch KAPOR将VISIPLOT / VITEREND销售给VisationC的母公司，个人软件，100万美元，并加入VISICALC的产品经理。
  1982年，KALT留下了将电子表格与图形组合的尚未命名的产品，以某种方式相信个人软件将产品雕刻出不竞争。 “我不确定为什么他们同意这一点，”他在一封电子邮件中写道，“也许他们觉得我缺乏剥夺这种雄心勃勃的东西的信誉。如果是这样，他们低估了我。“
 KAPOR于1982年创立了莲花，并于1983年推出1-2-3.在其第一年的运营中，莲花在收入和IPO的收入5300万美元。明年，它的收入增加了两倍至1.56亿美元。 SaaS已将离散销售取代为软件的Go-Busty Model，因为它对客户来说更好，产生了经常性收入，并且可以导致更高的终身价值，但没有SaaS公司曾尽快迅速地提出这么大的数字。
 同年KAPOR创立了莲花，比尔盖茨和微软帮派发布了它的第一个电子表格软件：Multiplan。使用R1C1寻址（行然后列）而不是A1（然后是我们习惯的列）是值得注意的，因为目标是成为最便携的电子表格应用程序，可在50多台不同的计算机上运行，​​而不是其他大量的电子表格应用程序。
 Lotus 1-2-3击碎的ullplan，微软返回绘图板与“项目奥德赛”。他们最初建立了奥德赛，比PC上的莲花1-2-3更好的电子表格，但在开发期间发生了两个关键的事情，这些事情会将项目转移到今天，36年后它仍然持有它。
 首先，球队的座右铭是：“重新计算或死亡。”据杰夫拉克斯，Mutiplan的产品经理和办公室背后的男人称，“一个名为Doug Klunder的精彩程序员弄清楚了如何同时两维进行计算算法，以便我们可以重新计算甚至比Lotus 1-2-3更快。” Klunder的创新意味着每次细胞发生变化时都必须重新计算每个细胞，而奥德赛仅重新计算受影响的细胞。这使其具有超过1-2-3的速度和性能优势，这创建了任何Excel用户熟悉的神奇体验：更改输入，并立即观看充满输出的工作表响应。
 其次，门和Raikes决定他们需要利用图形界面，因此它们从建筑物的Mid-Project转换为PC，该PC通过命令行界面运行，专门为Mac进行建立。 
在奥德赛工作的乔恩德文担任奥德赛的广泛用途：“这真的是当时的重要事项，将软件从博士学位模式带入普通人可以使用的东西。”
  凭借这两种创新，微软于1985年在Macintosh上推出Excel。正是在竞争对手的电脑上发布的反思决定，而Lotus 1-2-3则卡在自己的MS-DOS上，这将Excel陷入了主流。
 如果你想真的深入擅长Excel的历史，请观看整个视频：
  Excel迅速成为Mac上最流行的电子表格程序，然后是Microsoft第一个GUI OS上最受欢迎的Windows。它将Windows的增长率先通过收入（1991年）和运输单位（1992年）成为世界上最流行的电子表格软件。
  它没有回头。虽然今天难以爆发电子表格市场份额，因为Excel与办公室捆绑在一起，但Google的床单附带Guite，Excel占据了大多数估算的主导地位（〜80％+），近距离占据了更多密集使用案例，如金融模型。
 36年后，很难想象一个没有Excel的世界。如果明天擦掉地球的脸部，它可能会导致最大的损坏的单一应用。许多世界上最大的公司和金融机构依靠Excel模型来运行他们的业务，今天，Excel不仅仅是电子表格软件;这是一种语言。
  Excel是地球上最受欢迎的编程语言，大多数人在Excel中编程甚至没有意识到它们实际上是编程。估计有12亿人使用Microsoft Office，虽然很难确切地知道有多少人定期使用Excel，但估计将其达到7.5亿用户。相比之下，截至2018年，只有1070万只JavaScript开发商和700万个Python开发人员。 
Python和JavaScript，Excel之后的两个最受欢迎的编程语言，都是图灵完成;也就是说，它们可用于执行任何计算（以非常简化的术语）。另一方面，Excel直到最近没有完成。实际上，这意味着Excel根本不能用作许多类型的计算问题的“真实”编程语言的替代品，无论如何聪明地聪明都可以想到。
 （注意：VBA允许更多技术人员建立更多程序和自动化，但我们专注于Excel可以为较少的技术用户做些什么。）
 即使Excel不如语言专业开发人员使用，甚至其大多数用户都不认为自己是商业的程序员，也很难争辩在Excel中的工作不是编程。当您在Excel中的单元格中布局公式时，您正在使用一种功能语言。 Excel是功能的，因为它的公式（或函数）生成相同的精确输出，鉴于相同的输入，无论在您的电子表格或工作簿中是否发生了其他内容。您还可以链函数，将一个功能的输出传递为另一个功能，允许巨大数量的潜在计算管道。每次Excel都会增加函数，Excel的功率和灵活性乘以，因为可以将新功能链接到大量现有功能。
 所以如果在Excel工作是编程的，那么为什么它比其他语言更容易访问？
  Excel是声明性的，因为您可以通过键入公式来定义所需的内容，而无需担心如何执行逐步计算。我可以计算投资的返回内部返回（IRR），而无需了解公式，更不用说如何编程。我只是键入= err（c4：g4）和voila！
  通过每个更新到Excel的电子表格引擎，如何更快，更好地更好，没有用户举手。
 大多数传统语言都是较低的级别，这意味着程序员需要正式定义公式或功能需要执行的计算。不仅仅是=（isr ...），甚至不是完整的公式，而是这样做，那么这个，那么这个，那么这个，那么这个，那么这一点。根据这些计算的实施方式，性能，准确性和稳定性可能存在巨大后果 - 直接放在开发人员的肩部上的大负担。 
通过在非常高的抽象中运行，Excel用户被击败了处理大量细节和偶然细节的头痛，这些细节是令人恐惧和坦率地对大多数人不感兴趣的。相反，Microsoft分配了一支良好补偿的开发人员，以担心细节，并且用户只需要选择正确的功能。
  jakob nielson，标志性的用户体验设计师，定义了一个精神模型，作为“用户相信在手头系统的内容”。他规定，心理模型基于信仰，而不是事实，每个用户都有自己的心理模型。心理模型也易于惯性：“在用户中有巨大的惯性＆＃39;心理模型：人们知道趋于坚持的东西，即使它没有帮助。独自一人肯定是保守而不是新的互动风格的论点。“
 Excel利用了几十年来深入于我们的文化中深入的心理模型：使用A1表示法的二维网格。通过使用字母的数字和列分配行，用户可以在没有混淆或歧义的情况下识别大2D网格中的单个小区。通过粘贴到自1979年以来一直在使用的相同概念模型中，人们可以了解Excel如何在不学习任何新的情况下排列数据。
 该网格模型的持久性在Excel之外的LED应用程序采用相同或至少类似的模型，这反过来只加强了精神模型的笨拙，使其成为我们集体意识的永久性夹具。对于许多域来说，如果两个域名是最佳的，在工程师之间发生了热烈的辩论，但在绝大多数潜在用户中，它几乎无关紧要的技术圆圈。
  Excel最神奇的方面之一是它是反应性的。当您将输入更改为Excel中的公式时，任何取决于该输入的输出都会自动更新。因为Excel已经和我们在一起这么长时间，所以我们把这个财产视为理所当然。但是，大多数传统的编程语言都不是这样：当输入改变时，每个步骤取决于该输入需要故意重新运行输出以反映更改。
 通过反应性，Excel允许一种俏皮的交互。您可以使用输入和切换到工作簿，从而模拟不同的假设方案。对于好奇的令人享受，它可能是彻头彻尾的上瘾。但是，除了任何东西，反应性使得很容易获得非常快速的反馈，并且系统提供反馈的速度越快，要了解该系统的工作原理。 Excel旨在优化其用户在操作中培养技能的速度。
 收购技能需要定期的环境，有足够的机会练习，以及关于思想和行为的正确性的快速和明确的反馈。 
Excel用户不仅是不知不觉的程序员，它们也是不知不觉的完整堆栈程序员。 Excel工作簿可以是完全独立的，端到端的软件。一张纸可能包含数据库，另一张表可能包含一组公式来将表格与数据库转换，而另一页也可能是各种的用户界面。用户界面表可能会提供最终用户控件以操纵输入，同时也呈现最终输出的摘要数据和图表。
   另一块Excel魔术是能够检查和手动更新纸张中包含的数据库的条目。这不是大多数数据库的常态，它通常需要开发者技能和数据库管理员的权限来更新。
 通过自然完整的堆栈，一个人可以在Excel中构建一个复杂的模型，而无需依靠外部帮助。对于不宽松的任务来容易划分劳动力，这是一个基本的质量。投资银行家长期以来一直认为分析师和员工每周将在金融模式（在Excel中，当然）每周花费80至100小时的原因是他们的工作缺乏可分性;通常只有一个人拥有所有所需信息来构建模型。
 Excel结合了编程语言的力量，消费者软件的直接可用性，以及视频游戏的技能进展，灵活性适应几乎无限的用例。这是一个没有其他软件提供的组合，这就是为什么Excel能够生存和茁壮成长，而数百万其他应用已经出现并走了。
   Excel已经存在了很长时间，所以我们可以预期excel很长一段时间。
 这是林德效应在工作中：持续的速度越长，它将持续时间越长。预计已经存在一年的事情将在另一年左右，但是已经存在100年的东西预计将在另外100年内成为左右。
  质量。奶油上升到顶部，只有强烈的生存。部分林迪效应可以通过这些事实来解释，即一些东西比其他人更高的东西，人们认识和欣赏质量，而且随着时间的推移，更高的东西往往比较低于优质的东西。如果你把亚里士多德的尼古拉夫道德放在一个永久性的书架上，人们在持续的锦标赛中选择了它和一些现代的高小孩的哲学旋风，那么发电会认识到亚里士多德更好，亚里士多德的工作会生存。 
网络效果。随着人们认识到某些东西的质量，因为它持续更长时间，它们在它的顶部变得更加舒适，这增加了东西粘在周围的赔率。这是一种网络效果的形式，特别是双面平台网络效果。正如亚里士多德的工作仍然存在，更多的哲学家都在它上面建立，而且更多的哲学教授在其周围建造了课程，这会造成锁定，使其更有可能在千禧年幸存下来。
  在图形界面中引入了对电子表格的无缝反应性创造了卓越能够从Lotus 1-2-3窃取领先的神奇和直观的体验。随着它的发展，新的竞争对手试图窃取市场份额，最严重的谷歌表格，但在Excel的核心焦点区域做出严重分析工作的人不会梦想切换。 Excel太擅长了。它赢了，继续赢得质量。
 同时，Excel继续建立严重的网络效果：运行业务和市场的许多模型都是在Excel上建立的，开发人员为Excel，Banks和Consulting Comps培训Excel上的分析师培训分析师的插件，他们练习Excel非 - 停止多年并得到非常好的，当他们继续开始和运行公司时，他们要求使用Excel。它在公司之间也是可互操作的 - 您可以向世界上任何投资银行或对冲基金发送Excel电子表格，他们将能够开放和工作，这使得锁定更强。作为测试，选择您最喜欢的对冲基金分析师，发送给他们

  🎧  To get this essay straight in your ears: listen   here or on   Spotify or   Apple Podcast
    (Okay, not actually Michael Bolton. But he does star in this sponsor’s  latest ad campaign, so ... close enough.)
 I’ve talked ( and written) about   Public.com before, so you know the deal. One part investing app, one part community where you can discuss business trends and companies you believe in.
 Some of you might be looking for a new brokerage these days, so you should know that  Public is making the process of transferring your portfolio over ridiculously easy. They’re even covering the transfer fees from your old brokerage.
 For this week only,  download the app with my link and you’ll start with  $20 in free stock.
 You can even follow me and Michael Bolton there ( @packym and  @michaelbolton, respectively). Our music video drops soon 🔥

    One of the places I learn the most is a group chat I have with my friends  Dror Poleg and  Ben Rollert. Dror, who  writes about the history and future of work, cities, and finance, and Ben, who is the founder and CEO of  Composer, are two of the smartest people I know.
 Ben, the most technical of the trio (followed by Dror, then me), also happens to be an excellent writer. In late February, he released  The Composer Manifesto, to make the case for investing as a creative endeavor. It’s tailor made for the two types of people who read Not Boring -- investors and tech people -- and you should read it:
  So when Ben texted us about how underrated Excel’s power is, I asked him to write about it with me. He opened my eyes to so much depth I didn’t know existed in the product in which I spent every waking hour for the formative years of my career. We’ll try to do the same for you.
     In the popular marketing book   Alchemy, Rory Sutherland writes, “A spreadsheet leaves no room for miracles.” We could not disagree more strongly.
   But there’s one software product born in 1985, before many of us were even a twinkle in our parents’ eye, that inhabits its own category: it’s old, but we love it, we always will, and you’ll have to pry it from our cold, dead, fingers. That product, of course, is  Microsoft Excel.
  Anyone who has worked in finance or consulting grew up on it, learned to love it over thousands of hours of practice and improvement. Whether they realized it or not, they were becoming programmers, or at least no-code practitioners before the no-code movement took off. “Proficient in the Microsoft Office Suite” is so meaningless that it’s become a meme, but the ability to bend one specific Office program, Excel, to one’s will is a badge of honor.
 But the enduring, passionate user fervor for the product isn’t even its most unique attribute. Excel’s most lasting impact extends beyond the spreadsheet itself.
 Excel may be the most influential software ever built. It is a canonical example of  Steve Job’s bicycle of the mind, endowing its users with computational superpowers normally reserved for professional software engineers. Armed with those superpowers, users can create fully functional software programs in the form of a humble spreadsheet to solve problems in a seemingly limitless number of domains. These programs often serve as high-fidelity prototypes of domain specific applications just begging to be brought to market in a more polished form.
 If you want to see the future of B2B software, look at what Excel users are hacking together in spreadsheets today. Excel’s success has inspired the creation of software whose combined enterprise value dwarfs that of Excel alone. There are two main ways Excel has set the broad roadmap for the B2B software industry for decades, and will continue to for years to come:
 The Unbundling of Excel. Hundreds of B2B startups have been built by taking a job currently being done in Excel and trying to accomplish the job in more optimized, purpose-built B2B software. Every time you hear an entrepreneur say, “We’re replacing siloed spreadsheets and outdated processes with purpose-built software,” you’re hearing the Unbundling of Excel in real time. Many popular SaaS applications fall in this category. And yet, despite being “unbundled,” Excel keeps getting stronger.
 Inspired by Excel. That resiliency has inspired entrepreneurs to look more deeply at what makes Excel tick, and why. Adventurous builders are creating new software that doesn’t unbundle Excel, but is  Inspired by Excel. Excel’s balance of usability and flexibility can be found in popular no-code and low-code products created over three decades since Excel first graced the screen. This source of inspiration is less direct and more meta; it is less about recreating anything concrete that happens in Excel, and more about capturing the essence of what makes Excel so successful.
 We love Excel, everyone reading this probably loves Excel, and still, its impact is deeply underappreciated. Today, we’re going to fully appreciate it by covering:
    We have Steve Jobs to thank for Microsoft Excel, and Microsoft Excel to thank for Apple. Spreadsheet software was the first truly killer app for the Mac and home PC, and the Mac’s graphical interface helped bring spreadsheets to the masses. The two propelled each others’ growth.
 Excel wasn’t the first digital spreadsheet. When HBS student Dan Bricklin had to decide between doing spreadsheets for a case study by hand or on the school’s mainframe, he, like so many entrepreneurs, realized there had to be a better way. He launched VisiCalc, a “visible calculator,” in 1978. Computer Associates followed two years later in 1980 with SuperCalc. That same year, Mitch Kapor  sold VisiPlot/VisiTrend to VisiCalc’s parent company, Personal Software, for $1 million, and joined to work as a product manager on VisiCalc.
  In 1982, Kapor left to build a yet-to-be-named product that combined the spreadsheet with graphing, and somehow convinced Personal Software to carve the product out of his non-compete. “I am not sure why they agreed to this,” he  wrote in an email, “Perhaps they felt I lacked credibility to pull off something this ambitious. If so, they underestimated me.”
 Kapor founded Lotus in 1982 and launched 1-2-3 in 1983.  In its first year of operations, Lotus did $53 million in revenue and IPO’d. The next year, it tripled revenue to $156 million. SaaS has replaced discrete sales as the go-to business model for software because it’s better for the customer, generates recurring revenue, and can lead to a higher Lifetime Value, but no SaaS company has ever put up such big numbers as quickly as Lotus did.
 The same year Kapor founded Lotus, Bill Gates and the Microsoft gang released its first spreadsheet software: Multiplan. It was notable for using R1C1 addressing (row then column) instead of A1 (the column then row we’re used to), for aiming to be the most portable spreadsheet application, runnable on over 50 different computers, and not for much else.
 Lotus 1-2-3 crushed Multiplan, and Microsoft went back to the drawing board with “Project Odyssey.” They originally built Odyssey to be a better spreadsheet than Lotus 1-2-3 on the PC, but two critical things happened during development that would catapult the project into a lead that it still holds today, 36 years later.
 First, was the team’s motto: “Recalc or die.” According to Jeff Raikes, Mutiplan’s product manager and the man behind Office, “A brilliant programmer named Doug Klunder figured out how to do the  calculation algorithm in two dimensions simultaneously so that we could recalculate even faster than Lotus 1-2-3.” Klunder’s innovation meant that instead of having to recalculate (recalc)  every cell every time a cell changed, Odyssey only recalculated the affected cells. That gave it a huge speed and performance advantage over 1-2-3, which created the magical experience that any Excel user is familiar with: change an input, and watch worksheets full of outputs respond immediately.
 Second, Gates and Raikes decided that they needed to take advantage of the  graphical interface, so they switched mid-project from building for the PC, which was operated via command line interface, to  building exclusively for Mac.
 Jon Devaan, who worked on Odyssey, credited Jobs’ machine’s broad usability: “That was really the important thing at the time, to  bring software from PhD thesis mode into something that an average person could use.”
  With those two innovations, Microsoft launched Excel in 1985 exclusively on the Macintosh. It was that counterintuitive decision to launch on its competitor’s computer while Lotus 1-2-3 was stuck on its own MS-DOS, that brought Excel into the mainstream.
 If you want to go really deep on the history of Excel, watch this whole video:
  Excel quickly became the most popular spreadsheet program on the Mac, and then the most popular on Microsoft’s first GUI OS, Windows. It rode Windows’ growth to become the world’s most popular spreadsheet software by revenue (in 1991) and units shipped (in 1992).
  It hasn’t looked back. While it’s difficult to break out spreadsheet market share today since Excel comes bundled with Office and Google’s Sheets comes with GSuite, Excel holds a dominant position (~80%+) by most estimates, with a near monopoly for more intensive use cases like financial modeling.
 After 36 years, it’s hard to imagine a world without Excel. It’s likely the single application that would cause the most damage if it were wiped off the face of the earth tomorrow. Many of the world’s largest companies and financial institutions rely on Excel models to run their businesses, and today, Excel isn’t just a spreadsheet software; it’s a language.
  Excel is the most popular programming language on earth, and most people who program in Excel don’t even realize they are, in fact, programming. There are an  estimated 1.2 billion people who use Microsoft Office, and while it’s hard to know exactly how many people use Excel regularly, estimates put it at  750 million users. By comparison, as of 2018, there were only  10.7 million Javascript developers and 7 million Python developers.
 Python and Javascript, the two most popular programming languages after Excel, are both  Turing complete; that is, they can be used to perform any computation (in very simplified terms). Excel, on the other hand, was not Turing complete until very recently. In practical terms, this means that Excel simply could not be used as a substitute for a “true” programming language for many types of computational problems, no matter how clever the hacks a power user might think of.
 (Note: VBA allows more technical people to build even more programs and automations, but we’re focused on what Excel can do for less technical users.)
 Even if Excel is not as powerful as the languages professional developers use, and even if most of its users do not consider themselves programmers by trade, it’s hard to argue that working in Excel  isn’t programming. When you layout formulas in cells in Excel, you are working with a kind of functional language. Excel is  functional in that its formulas (or functions) generate the same exact output, given the same input, no matter what else is happening in your spreadsheet or workbook. You can also  chain functions, passing the output of one function as the input to another, allowing for an enormous number of potential computational pipelines. Each time Excel adds a function, the power and flexibility of Excel is multiplied, since that new function can be chained to a large number of existing functions.
 So if working in Excel is programming, why is it so much more accessible than other languages?
  Excel is declarative in that you define  what you want by typing a formula, without having to worry about  how to perform the step-by-step computations. I can calculate the Internal Rate of Return (IRR) on an investment without needing to know the formula, let alone how to program it. I just type =IRR(C4:G4) and voila!
  With each update to Excel’s spreadsheet engine, the  how gets faster and better, without the user having to lift a finger.
 Most conventional languages are lower level, meaning that the programmer needs to formally define the computations that a formula or function needs to perform. Not just =(IRR…), not even just the full formula, but do this, then this, then this, then this, then this, then this. Depending on how these computations are implemented, there can be huge consequences for performance, accuracy, and stability - a large burden placed directly on the shoulders of the developer.
 By operating at a very high level of abstraction, an Excel user is spared the headache of dealing with a lot of minutiae and incidental detail that is intimidating and frankly uninteresting to most people. Instead, Microsoft assigns an army of well-compensated developers to worry about the details, and the user just has to pick the right function to use.
  Jakob Nielson, the iconic User Experience designer, defines a mental model as “what the user believes about the system at hand.” He stipulates that mental models are based on belief, not facts, and each user has their own mental model. Mental models are also susceptible to inertia: “There's great inertia in users' mental models: stuff that people know well tends to stick, even when it's not helpful. This alone is surely an argument for being conservative and not coming up with  new interaction styles.”
 Excel leverages a mental model that has been deeply ingrained in our culture for decades: a two dimensional grid using  A1 notation. By assigning rows with numbers and columns with letters, a user can identify a single cell in a large 2D grid without confusion or ambiguity. By sticking to the same conceptual model that has been in use since at least 1979, people can understand how Excel arranges data without learning anything new.
 The persistence of this grid model has led applications outside of Excel to adopt the same or at least a similar model, which in turn only reinforces the ubiquity of the mental model, making it a permanent fixture of our collective consciousness. Whether a 2D grid is optimal for many domains is hotly debated amongst engineers, but it’s almost irrelevant outside of technical circles given its inertia amongst the vast majority of potential users.
  One of the most magical aspects of Excel is that it is  reactive. When you change an input to a formula in Excel, any output that depends on that input is automatically updated. Because Excel has been with us for so long, we take this property for granted. But most conventional programming languages are not like this: when an input is changed, each step that depends on that input needs to be deliberately re-run for the output to reflect the change.
 By being reactive, Excel allows for a kind of playful interactivity. You can play with inputs and toggles to a workbook, simulating different hypothetical scenarios. For the insatiability curious, it can be downright addictive. But more than anything, reactivity makes it easy to get very fast feedback, and the faster a system provides feedback, the easier it is to understand how that system works. Excel is designed to optimize the speed at which its users develop skill at operating it.
 Acquisition of skills requires a regular environment, an adequate opportunity to practice, and  rapid and unequivocal feedback about the correctness of thoughts and actions.
  Excel users are not only unwittingly programmers, they’re also unwittingly full stack programmers. An Excel workbook can be an entirely self-contained, end-to-end piece of software. One sheet might contain a database, another sheet might contain a set of formulas to transform the sheet with the database, and another sheet might be a user interface of sorts. The user interface sheet might offer the end user controls to manipulate inputs, while also presenting summary data and charts of the final outputs.
   Another piece of Excel magic is the ability to inspect and manually update the entries of a database contained in a sheet. This is just not the norm with most databases, which typically require developer skills and permissions of a database administrator to update.
 By being naturally full stack, a single person can build a complex model in Excel without needing to rely on outside help. And for tasks that don’t lend themselves to easy division of a labor, this is an essential quality. Investment Bankers have long argued the reason that analysts and associates will spend 80 to 100 hours a week on financial models (in Excel, of course) is the lack of divisibility of their work; often only one person has all the needed information to build the model.
 Excel combines the power of a programming language, the immediate usability of consumer software, and the skill progression of a video game with the flexibility to adapt to nearly infinite use cases. That’s a combination no other software offers, and it’s why Excel has been able to survive and thrive while millions of other applications have come and gone.
   Excel has been around a long time, so we can expect Excel to be around for a long time.
 That’s the  Lindy Effect at work: the  longer something lasts, the longer it can be expected to last. Something that has been around for a year is expected to be around for another year, but something that has been around for 100 years is expected to be around for another 100 years.
  Quality. Cream rises to the top, and only the strong survive. Part of the Lindy Effect can just be explained by the fact that some things are higher-quality than others, that people recognize and appreciate quality, and that over time, higher-quality things tend to outlast lower-quality things. If you put Aristotle’s  The Nicomachean Ethics on a permanent bookshelf and had people choose between it and some modern high kid’s philosophical ramblings in an ongoing tournament, generation after generation would recognize that Aristotle is better, and Aristotle’s work would survive.
 Network Effects. As people recognize something’s quality and as it lasts longer, they become more comfortable building on top of it, which increases the odds that the thing sticks around. That’s a form of network effect, specifically a  Two-Sided Platform Network Effect. As Aristotle’s work persists, more philosophers build on top of it, and more philosophy professors build their curricula around it, which creates lock-in and makes it even more likely that his work survives for millennia hence.
  Introducing seamless reactivity to spreadsheets in a graphical interface created such a magical and intuitive experience that Excel was able to steal the lead from Lotus 1-2-3. As it’s evolved, new competitors have tried to steal market share, most seriously Google Sheets, but those doing serious analytical work in Excel’s core focus area wouldn’t dream of switching. Excel is too good at what it does. It won, and continues to win, on quality.
 Meanwhile, Excel continues to build up serious network effects: many of the models that run businesses and markets are built on Excel, developers build plug-ins for Excel, banks and consulting firms train incoming classes of analysts on Excel, they practice Excel non-stop for years and get really good, and when they go on to start and run companies, they mandate the use of Excel. It’s also interoperable between firms -- you can send an Excel spreadsheet to any investment bank or hedge fund in the world and they’ll be able to open and work in it, which makes the lock-in stronger. As a test, pick your favorite hedge fund analyst, send them

来自工作和生活在刑事司法系统的人的观点。注册收到＆＃34;里面的生活和＃34;每周都给你发电子邮件。
  不久前我的母亲告诉我，由于“女王的康马特”，国际象棋如何获得新的人气，这是一个以基本开放命名的Netflix戏剧。我是一个狂热的国际象棋球员，喜欢把自己想象成一个有点强大的对手。我的主要反对者是沃利，一个特别有天赋的球员，其中棋子纹身在他的指关节上。在他的追求中练习才能成为一个祖父，他一直努力地击败了我。
  我不介意失去沃利，但在Covid-19流行期间在监狱中是最糟糕的。这就像是在一个俄罗斯筑娃娃之一的中心 - 一个盒子里的一个盒子里的一个盒子。社会疏远政策限制了我们进入娱乐码，日间，日，课程和手机。有些日子我们在我们的6×10个细胞中花了大约23个小时。在一切之上，包括棋盘游戏 - 包括国际象棋 - 已被完全禁止促进社会疏远。这种情况强迫我们创造了一个经典游戏的新方法。
  在“允许的董事会游戏”签名后几天，发布了几天，似乎沃利的追求成为宏大的追求将是无限期的。但威廉和我不是放弃梦想的类型。
 幸运的是，对于我们而言，对于我们的邻居来说，我们生活在同一个行，两个细胞分开。由于我们彼此喊叫，我们决定在各自的细胞中玩游戏。我们都建立了国际象棋棋盘，并使用了代数符号来告诉 - 我们彼此的举动。然后我们在董事会上移动了相应的碎片。
 要了解我们的游戏如何对我们的邻近邻居响起，请简要说明国际象棋棋盘。电路板上的列由字母A到H引用。行由数字1到8引用。因此，板上的每个广场都有自己的字母和数字，我们用来描述碎片移动的位置。所以，如果我向C6移动主教，我说，“主教C6”。 （对于典当，它只是“C6”。）
 但监狱是响亮的，而字母B，C，D和E声音在Ruckus上非常相似。为了避免混乱，我想出了那些字母的命名法：“鳄鱼”为A，“棒球”为B，“Constellation”为C，“恐龙”为D，“大象”为E和“高尔夫球” G。 
我们成了笑话的屁股，因为我们听起来有多荒谬，“恐龙4！”但嘲笑没有打扰我们。我们只是妨碍了这种游戏，以这种方式花了至少三个小时。
 第二天，Wally和我在日间在一次在一次几小时坐在金属桌子上。我们回忆着我们如何在那个桌子上播放国际象棋，并且就像我最荒谬的想法一样出生。 “为什么我们不只是在我们的头脑中发挥作用？”我向50％的讽刺问道。我不认真，但沃莱·盖住我的想法就像鱼一样勾拳。
 自大流行开始以来一直失踪的喜悦感，我们开始了我们的第一场精神棋。我们每个人都搬家了，让我们的轴承。我们播放，直到一名军官大喊大叫，“架起它！”我们的一小时结束了。
 尽管沃利人民远离我的人远远不到20英尺，但我唯一有机会看到他的其他时间是他们让我们出去吃饭。当我晚些时候我在自助餐厅追赶他时，他嘴里的第一个言语是，“你还记得一切都在哪里吗？” “我当然，”我回答道。 “轮到你了。”
 我们再次开始玩，我们面临的间隔表达捕获了几个囚犯附近的好奇心。很明显，我们在一起做点什么，但我们不说话。然后Wally宣称，“骑士在大象7上捕获，检查！”我说，“男人，那是一个很好的举动！我甚至没有看到它！“男人看着我们，就像我们完全是脾气暴躁的第二次，然后就他们的生意。这对沃利和我来说是热闹的。我们喜欢超越时间和空间的文字障碍，并以创造力的方式挑战自己。
    现在沃利和我在日间开始我们的游戏，在午餐时继续下一天，并在三天后完成我们的细胞。这让我在日间提供了最新的比赛。
 “恐龙4！”沃利开始了。他带领我进入女王＆＃39; s gambit，一个非常有效的开放。我们坐在金属长凳上，其中一个螺栓固定在电视前的混凝土地板中。有人坐在Wally问的另一边，“这是什么电影？”我们都不回应。那个男人更坚持，“嘿，电视上的电影是什么？” 
进入入侵，沃利回答说：“我不知道，我不是在看。”
 “你是什么意思？”那家伙要求。 “你盯着它。”
 “我真的在下棋，”沃利说。打手势他补充说：“我们在我们思想中互相互相争夺。”
 我把这个家伙给了一个证实的点头，所以我的朋友听起来不完全疯狂。 Wally继续描述我们如何想象董事会以跟踪彼此的移动。事实证明，该男子自己是棋手。他有兴趣了解我们的游戏如何走。如此沃利和我继续玩耍，只谈论，才能使我们的举动或澄清我们的观众的奇怪术语。
 一个男人在替补席上问我们的观众，“你在做什么，'兄弟吗？”他回答道，“我正在看着他们棋。”另一个男人看着我们，然后回到他的家庭。 “你是......什么？”我为我造成了认真的努力来压抑我的笑声并保持专注。到了一小时结束时，我被谦卑，鼓励看到更多的人受到我们的想法，并希望尝试他们的手 - 或者我应该在一场比赛中说出他们的大脑。
 可能是一厢情愿的人说，我们很快就会有一场精神上的国际象棋锦标赛，但随着古怪的广场名称和纯粹的挑战，肯定是趋势。更重要的是，我们在孤独的沙漠中间创建的这种知识产青树将人们从各种文化和背景中带到一起。在我们的心理棋牌游戏的核心上，这是一个深刻的课：很容易在生活中发挥受害者，让你的情况决定你的性格。但是内容或心烦意乱的差异是一种观点的问题。我们每个人都有能力培养我们想要的角度，并将其应用于我们的经验。虽然我们被迫放弃了我们的身体自由，但我们不必放弃对我们的思想来控制。谁知道？也许这一天之一我终于击败了Wally。
 Harlin Pierce，24，是歌手，歌曲作者，视觉艺术家和作家。在新墨西哥的圣诞老人Fe，原生在高中毕业之前不久被监禁。皮尔斯对学习的热情继续，具有数学，语言和身体健康的独立研究。他还教导了被监禁的人们音乐，并参加了他成立的书籍俱乐部。 Pierce正在德克萨斯州吉姆弗格森单位为谋杀案33年。

Perspectives from those who work and live in the criminal justice system.  Sign up to receive "Life Inside" emailed to you every week.
  Not too long ago my mother told me about how chess has gained fresh popularity due to “The Queen’s Gambit,” a hit Netflix drama named after a fundamental opening. I’m an avid chess player and like to think of myself as a somewhat formidable opponent. My main adversary is Wally, an especially gifted player with chess pieces tattooed on his knuckles. In his quest to practice enough to become a grandmaster, he beats me consistently.
  I don’t mind losing to Wally, but being in prison during the COVID-19 pandemic is the worst. It’s like being at the center of one of those Russian nesting dolls—a box within a box within a box. Social distancing policies limit our access to recreation yards, the dayroom, classes and phones. Some days we spend roughly 23 hours in our 6-by-10 cells. And on top of everything, board games—including chess—have been completely banned to promote social distancing. This situation has forced us to create a new approach to a classic game.
  For a few desolate days after the “No Board Games Allowed” sign was posted, it seemed that Wally’s quest to become a grandmaster would be on hold indefinitely. But Wally and I are not the type to give up on our dreams.
 Fortunately for us—and unfortunately for our neighbors—we live on the same row, two cells apart. Since we’re in shouting distance of one another, we decided to play the game in our respective cells. We both set up our chess boards and used algebraic notations to tell—or yell—our moves to one another. Then we moved the corresponding pieces on our boards.
 To understand how our game sounded to our disgruntled neighbors, a brief explanation of the chess board is necessary. The columns on the board are referenced by the letters A to H. The rows are referenced by the numbers 1 to 8. So every square on the board has its own letter and number that we use to describe where the pieces move. So if I move a bishop to c6, I say, “bishop c6.” (For pawns it would just be “c6.”)
 But prison is loud, and the letters B, C, D and E sound very similar over a ruckus. To avoid confusion Wally and I came up with a nomenclature for those letters: “alligator” for A, “baseball” for B, “constellation” for C, “dinosaur” for D, “elephant” for E and “golf ball” for G.
 We became the butt of jokes because of how ridiculous we sounded yelling out moves like, “dinosaur 4!” But the ridicule didn’t bother us. We were only deterred by the fact that playing the game this way took at least three hours.
 The following day, Wally and I were sitting together at a metal table during a measly hour in the dayroom. We were reminiscing about how we used to be able to play chess at that very table, and just like that one of my most absurd ideas to date was born. “Why don’t we just play in our heads?” I asked Wally with 50 percent sarcasm. I wasn’t serious, but Wally latched on to my idea like a fish to a hook.
 With a sense of joy that had been missing since the pandemic started, we began our first game of mental chess. We each made a few moves, getting our bearings. We played until an officer yelled, “Rack it up!” Our hour was over.
 Despite the fact that Wally lives fewer than 20 feet away from me, the only other time I would have a chance to see him is when they let us out for a meal. When I caught up to him in the cafeteria later that day, the first words out of his mouth were, “Do you remember where everything is?” “Of course I do,” I replied. “It’s your turn.”
 We started playing again, and the spaced out expressions on our faces captured the curiosity of a few inmates nearby. It was obvious we were doing something together, but we weren’t talking. Then Wally declared, “Knight captures on elephant 7, check!” And I said, “Man, that was a great move! I didn’t even see it!” The men looked at us like we were totally nuts for a split second then went about their business. It was hilarious to Wally and me. We loved transcending the literal barriers of time and space and challenging ourselves in a creative way.
    Now Wally and I start our games in the dayroom, continue the next day at lunch, and finish three days later by yelling from our cells. Which leads me to our most recent game in the dayroom.
 “Dinosaur 4!” Wally began. He was leading me into the Queen's Gambit, a very effective opening. We were sitting on a metal bench, one of many bolted to the concrete floor in front of the TV. Someone sitting on the other side of Wally asked, “What movie is this?” Neither of us responded. The man asked more insistently, “Hey, what movie is on TV?”
 Giving in to the intrusion, Wally replied, “I don’t know, I’m not watching it.”
 “What do you mean?” the guy demanded. “You’re staring right at it.”
 “I’m actually playing chess,” Wally said. Gesturing toward me he added, “We’re playing against each other in our minds.”
 I gave the guy a corroborating nod so my friend didn’t sound totally crazy. Wally went on to describe how we visualize the board to keep track of each other’s moves. It turned out that the man was a chess player himself. He was interested in seeing how our game would go. So Wally and I continued playing, only talking to make our moves or to clarify our odd nomenclature for our spectator.
 A man farther down the bench asked our spectator, “What are you doin,’ bro?” He responded, “I’m watching them play chess.” The other man looked at us, and then back to his homeboy. “You’re… what?” It took a serious effort for me to suppress my laughter and stay focused. By the end of the hour, I was humbled and encouraged to see that several more guys had become intrigued by our idea and wanted to try their hand—or should I say their brain—at a game.
 It may be wishful thinking to say that we’ll have a mental chess tournament anytime soon, but with the quirky square names and the sheer challenge, it’s certainly trending. More importantly, this intellectual oasis we created in the middle of a lonely desert brings people from diverse cultures and backgrounds together. At the heart of our mental chess game lies a profound lesson: It is easy to play the victim in life, to allow your circumstances to dictate your disposition. But the difference between being content or distraught is a matter of perspective. Every one of us has the ability to cultivate the perspective we want for ourselves and apply it to our experience. While we have been forced to relinquish our physical freedom, we don’t have to give up control over our minds. And who knows? Maybe one of these days I’ll finally beat Wally.
 Harlin Pierce, 24, is a singer, songwriter, visual artist and writer. The Santa Fe, New Mexico, native became  incarcerated  shortly before he was due to graduate from high school. Pierce’s passion for learning continues, with independent studies in math, languages and physical fitness.  He also teaches fellow incarcerated people music and participates in a book club he founded. Pierce is serving 33 years for murder at the Jim Ferguson Unit, Texas.
title: 当监狱被禁止的棋盘游戏时，我们在我们的思想中发挥了象棋
title_en: When the prison banned board games, we played chess in our minds

很多人都在问我即将到来的书，关于艰难的东西的难过，就是这样。这是我为这本书写作的一件，没有做出削减。我仍然认为这是一个非常好的故事，给你一个味道。
 我只是说实话，所以我在每一个引擎盖21岁的地方都很酷，我永远不会遇到一个好的警察 - 德拉克，“我走了”
  在从LoudCloud转变为Opsware后，我们需要一个财务负责人。因此，当来自一个最佳企业软件公司之一的首席财务官变得可用时，我跳起来有机会雇用她。
 Michelle（注意：她的名字已经改变）全面了解软件会计，商业模式和最佳实践，并且由于她以前的公司业务的诚实和直接的报告，她没有小部分的华尔街。在我的参考检查中，至少有十几名投资者告诉我，当数字失望时，他们的数量比公司表现令人失望了更多的钱，因为他们表示，当她说事情比他们出现并在逢低买到的东西时，他们相信米歇尔。
 一旦她出入船上，米歇尔迅速审查了我们所有的实践和流程，以确保我们既符合符合和竞争力。她认为我们不如竞争力的一个区域是我们的股票期权授予进程。她据报道，她以前的公司在该月期间将股票期权价格设定的股票期权价格授予员工的股票期权比我们的员工更有利。她还表示，由于公司的外部法律顾问设计并由审计师批准，它完全符合法律。
 这一切都听起来很棒：没有额外的成本或风险的员工更好的激励。然而，经过近四年的灾难性的惊喜，没有什么能让我更加紧张，而不是听起来很棒。最重要的是，与会计法有关的变化总是担心我。
 他们担心我，因为我们作为公司所做的每一个激励都旨在鼓励人们实现目标。所有这些激励措施都有警告，即遵守法律时必须达到目标。现在可能听起来很简单，但几乎每天都会每天都会讨论他们的目标以及他们将如何实现它们。他们几乎从不讨论会计法。在销售预测会议中，您经常会听到，“我们可以在本季度结束时完成，以便将其关闭？”你从来没有听到过，“我们将承诺符合第97-2（关键软件会计规则）遵守陈述（关键软件会计规则）吗？” 
除此之外，美国会计法很难理解，往往是不合逻辑和随机的。例如，有关股票期权的法律，FAS 123，填写如下所示：
 “本声明未指定与非雇员的股票支付交易的测量日期，其中收到的货物费用的衡量标准基于已发行的股权文书的公允价值。 EITF问题第96-18号，“根据雇员以外的雇员或与卖出，商品或服务结合签发的股票工具，建立了确定股票股​​票股票股票计量日期的标准非员工的交易。“
  用两种广泛的措施，故意或不小心地违反法律，以追求目标。首先，当我们开始公司时，Marc和我同意该公司的一般律师总是将直接向我汇报。这与许多技术公司不同，总律师向首席财务官报告。这样，另一个高管将无法颠覆追求该人数的法律。其次，我经常向那样发表这项财务员工：
 “在这项业务中，我们可能会遇到麻烦。我们可能会错过一个季度。我们甚至可能会破产，但我们不会入狱。所以，如果有人要求你做一些你认为可能让你入狱的事情，请给我打电话。“
 随着它的背景，我告诉米歇尔，一个更好的股票授予过程听起来很棒，但我需要乔丹布雷斯洛，我的总法律顾问，在做出决定之前审查它。乔丹住在伯克利的家乡，他当然属于那里。随着嬉皮士的敏感，约旦对公司政治，展示或任何涉及真理的行为几乎过敏。结果，我知道他所说的是100％他相信的东西，与其他任何事情无关。我可以相信它。米歇尔很惊讶，因为她以前的公司多年来一直在普罗斯水下的普罗斯在会计师事务所的普华永道批准这一惯例。我说：“这一切都很好，但我仍然需要约旦先审查它。”
 乔丹回到了一个我没想到的答案：“本，我已经过了六次法律，并没有办法在法律的范围内严格。我不确定PWC如何证明它，但我建议对抗它。“我告诉米歇尔，我们不会实施政策，这就是那样。
 好吧，这是一段时间。然后，差不多两年后，秒宣布它正在调查米歇尔以前的股票期权会计违规行为。这开始大规模调查所有硅谷公司及其股票期权会计实践。总而言之，有超过200家公司被判有某种不规则的罪。 
2005年11月，米歇尔的以前的雇主宣布，它在录取了不法行为时删除了大多数管理团队。证券交易所发布了米歇尔的井通知，这是一封信，指出，它计划立即建立执法行动。这不是一个起诉书，但这是一个正式的调查，它会非常分散注意力。我不得不要求她下台。在某些方面，选择是显而易见的 - 我们无法将整个公司置于一个人的风险。尽管如此，在Opsware在OPSWARE中没有任何不对的人射击是艰难的。尽管如此，米歇尔慷慨地辞职，因为她不想带来负面关注公司。
 在遵循的日子里，我仔细定位了对公司的改变，并没有把米歇尔放在糟糕的光线上。我告诉我们的员工，会计欺诈和会计错误之间存在差异，我相信米歇尔对她以前的公司犯了犯错，但没有犯欺诈。我向我们的投资者解释道，他们喜欢米歇尔，我也非常高度其想，但我别无选择。公司先得。
 Michelle最终为她在其他公司的股票期权练习中为3个半月提供了3个半月 - 我们几乎在Opsware实现的同样的做法。由于我们有同样的财务负责人，我们几乎肯定会被调查。我显然不知道在其他公司发生了什么，但我知道米歇尔没有打算破坏任何法律，不知道她会破坏任何法律。整件事是一个古老的谚语：“当稻谷的马车拉到生病的房子时，你在做什么并不重要。每个人都在监狱。“一旦证券证局决定大多数技术公司股票期权程序，监狱句是任意发行的。
 回想起来，让我失去监狱的唯一幸运的是好运和一个杰出的总法律顾问，以及合适的组织设计。
